item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is easily one of my favorite SCPs because of like, the heart-wrenching value of it, y'know? It tells a clear story, and is just. Absolutely excellent in delivering it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good article, disturbing and sad at the same time.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, that SCP shows one of the limit of SCPs. The idea that Foundation would never use child is probably really deep. But I think… why not? Yes, it's bad and on the beggining is hinted that they do not do more experiments for that reason. But if Foundation needs informations about that type of SCP, they have to do it. A least one. But it's so disturbing that many authors do not write about it. Upvote.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure this has been mentioned already but… How can SCP-1357-A prevent access for those without -B when the -As only get activated when conditions are correctly met

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like many others in this thread, I'm a bit torn on this. On the one hand, it does make little sense as to why the Foundation would purposefully put a child who lives outside the veil in danger of an anomaly inside the veil. I mean, no canon and all that, but this is stretching the boundaries of what a sensible Foundation would do.

On the other hand, this is just so scary. There's so much adult fear in this, and the worst part is that it actively seeks you out with the tickets. The thought of something like this existing is just a scary thought, because you don't know what's happening on the inside.

For making me feel an emotion and making me wonder, you get a +1, albeit a less enthusiastic +1 than if a more sensible procedure was conducted for testing (I'm not really sure how one would go about doing that, and the author has been inactive for more than 3 years now so who knows)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ;;_;; This actually made me cry a little :(

item.title: Thanks I hate it
item.content:encoded:
I came here for science, not feels…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think in this case they would actually try another way to retrieve the agent's daughter, or would amnesticize the couple. The Foundation also demonstrated several times they can think of more creative ways to interact with anomalies that follow strange rules.

Overall I found several issues with the skip, but the final punch is really a hell of a delivery. And yet, I think in its current state it would work better as a tale than a skip, or those issues should be addressed, from inadequate Object Class (this clearly isn't Safe, but Euclid. It demands continual effort to contain and still interacts with the outside world), to not that much in terms of clinical language.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate these bastards. May Playland burn into nothingness after the Foundation disappears.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be the only SCP to make me feel angry and sad, and I love it. I do wish tho, that their was more to this so then my brain could stop thinkng of all the possibilties and why they capture children and kill them or transform them. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was quite good +1

I think you made a mistake:

"Don't worry sweetie, you don't have to leave if you don't have to! They'll be back for you later, when you're ready."

The second 'have to' was probably meant to be 'want to', unless it's a Blue's Clues reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now i have the angry tears. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What, i am not crying, This is just rain that is only landing on my face. I enjoyed reading the skip, but what happened to the daughter… I cant explain how much this made me angry-sad. Over all this is Amazingly well written but i'm going to my corner to sulk now. >;(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I love about this one the most is not the SCP in and of itself, which is scary enough.

It's Hendricks. Poor, poor Hendricks, who must have loved his daughter dearly and has lost her forever because he was wedded to his work. It really cranks up the Adult Fear and the tearjerker effects, especially at the end when he begs for his daughter back. I wish there were more SCPs like this that demonstrate just how traumatic they can be for people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why are the SCP instances letting Hailey, presumably still a child at that point, answer the phones for Playland? Seems like a cheap/lazy plot device. I liked it just enough not to downvote .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This pissed me off a little and I'm going to go write fanfiction about the GOC destroying it to make myself feel better.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I'm never taking my daughter to an amusement park again

Take your +1
you monster

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The payoff that's gone for in here is wonderful, but in order to get to that emotional payoff and heartwrenching, you suspend so much disbelief and take great strides to dumb down the Foundation in order to get to this payoff that it reads so forced and becomes nonsensical. Every time you try to get me invested, you intend to go a step forward to justify that, and instead accidentally jump backwards. Even if the focus isn't on the Foundation, how this thing is contained, and what the Foundation does in order to set everything in place for your heartwrenching moments (and this article is admittedly good at that, and suitably creepy to boot), there still has to be enough in here to make sense. And the in-universe logic here makes me wonder how the Foundation was able to get anywhere with containing stuff when they were emotionally driven to let someone directly affected by the anomaly to oversee it (with disastrous results as well).

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way I read it they didn't kill her, but rather transformed her into one of them

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why people are calling this shocking, it's really predictable, and incredibly cheap.

Anyone who couldn't predict that they weren't going to let the daughter go and would then kill her has never read an SCP. The parts that aren't purely predictable paint-by-numbers emotional manipulation are the parts that don't make sense re: the personnel incompetence shown by the Foundation here. And they wouldn't be as bad if the story they were built around wasn't so cheap. And it's REALLY obvious that the MTF fails because the SCP, whatever it is, always has to totally repel any attempts to mess with it, no matter what they are, regardless if they make any sense or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda surprised I didn't see this pointed out in the comments, but "vary in age, ethnicity, sex, and gender."

"…sex, and gender."

+1 for all my sads and many of my nightmares. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about this is compelling, but too much emphasis is placed on "where's my daughter I want my daughter back!" that it becomes painful on many levels — the emotional empathetic level, and the "This keeps going on and on and I've already forgotten what the anomaly is even supposed to be", and the "Shut up already"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah, and I thought 1981 was scary. As someone who watched Blue's Clues a lot as a kid, this is REALLY frickin' creepy. I wish someone could do a reading of the recorded dialogue. Oh, and then there's this little gem.

"Notable characters that instances of SCP-1357-A have shown costumed as include Winnie the Pooh, Barney the Dinosaur, and Batman."

Imagine it. Being attacked by hundreds of Batmans. That's just awesome. +1 to you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one epic article. and the picture was hilarious. (well for me anyways).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mother of god…

I could tell from the pic and start of description that it was a good old fashioned park- attendants- turn-you into- one- of- them story, but the way you did it. .. the pacing,the subtle hints, the Goddamn implications. .. that's what made it one of the few articlesOn the entire site that genuinely gave me the chills. Major props.

Edit: pardon the typos, typing on my phone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, since the only requirement for Playland's effects to activate are that there be a family with a minor visit it, what would happen if, say, a family with 14 year-old obsessed with Halo, Batman, and/or Warhammer 40000 were to enter the park?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just kept getting creepier and creepier as I continued down the page. Definitely nightmare material right here. I've got to put this on my list of things to never read again once I've become a father.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god that was just…

Wow, something about those logs just made me queasy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well damn. I like the pacing of this extremely well. The actual malicious aspect isn't introduced right off the bat, and it just gets creepier and creepier at a pace where it sort of crawls up on you.
As for the idea itself- I feel as if the guardians would know something's up if every single person there is dressed as the same exact character- no other people there for recreation, only workers? However, I suppose that could be one of the 'tells' that leads the parents to try to get themselves and their kids out of there that ends in the child's kidnap.

Questions, though- is every child who ever enters kidnapped or is that just when the parent's don't make sure the child is happy, as the researcher's case was?
Also, what happens if multiple parties of three (two guardians one child) enter with the children loving different characters? It does say that people can receive up to six tickets.
Still though, one of the better ones. +1

item.title: SCP-1357
item.content:encoded:
NUKE IT FROM SPACE!!

No, but seriously, I don't see how that agent hasn't gone on more killing sprees in that park. Man has got to be rather messed up from all of this. This is why you never get your family involved with the Foundation!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good god, this is sad. Upvoted, you bastard. >;'(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All my sads. They hurt. ;_;

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just… amazing! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It did make me feel physically ill at the end, and the setup is very memorable, so I upvoted.

I do wish that it didn't require the Foundation to be totally unforgivable morons to make the situation in the logs happen though. /sigh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one was a lot more "meh" for me than other people, I guess. The core idea of something twisted that wants to entertain children and steal them away has been done before and better in plenty other media. Pinocchio is one of the older examples that spring to mind.

There's nothing really interesting that grabbed me, and as soon as the child is separated from the parents it's pretty obvious where the whole thing is headed, at which point the logs are really redundant and predictable. I think it would have been much better to have left them all out and instead dropped a few small, mysterious hints about her fate, leaving the reader to fill in the blanks. The play-by-play doesn't do anything unexpected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saw it coming. Horrified me all the same. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fridge Logic aside, it's a rare SCP that can make me feel physically ill by the end. I upvoted when I saw the Legion of Pooh Bears, and my stomach is keeping me from changing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On top of what Smapti said, it runs counter to one of the other tenants of Foundation: secrecy. It's really obvious that something's going on if a huge proportion of the top researchers in almost every field get accused of child pornography and then vanish mysteriously. Hell, if the Foundation did do that, I could see a researcher who joined without that starting to think that there's some anomalous force targeting researchers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case, the Foundation is presented here as completely incompetent by stationing a response team that they should have known would be too small to do anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The US Army figured out after Vietnam that conscripts aren't any good for anything other than holding a rifle. You cannot establish a professional cadre of specialists out of people who've been forced to be there, let alone reward them for their loyalty by threatening their family. If the Foundation were made up of people who'd been blackmailed into taking and keeping the job, I'd expect to see rampant desertion, low morale, zero work ethic, and regular containment breaches because, hey, why should they care, they're dead no matter whether they do the job or not.

And again - what happens if the wife says no? Do they fire him? Do they kidnap her and force her to go? Does the wife have any free will at all, or does the fact that her husband took a Foundation job mean she's obligated to stay in the kitchen and do as he says?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clearly, the agent and his family are artificially manufactured homunculi, and the Foundation is crippled by abyssal judgement.

/headcanon

item.title: Re: hey guys
item.content:encoded:
Ah, I see, you meant "head canon", gotcha.
I don't use the terms interchangably, which is why I got confused. But a lot of people do, and that's cool too.
Never mind me, then. All my points on the matter are already up-thread.

item.title: Re: hey guys
item.content:encoded:
I'd argue that it does break the canon. Whether it does it a little or a lot is up to how much headcanon you have built up, I guess, but I'd definitely claim that it does.

1) Sending the agent and his own family in. I don't actually mind this so much; I think it's dumb, but I can write it off as something like maybe the agent volunteering, his superiors going "well, okay, why not?" and so on.
2) Having the SCP do something bad to the agent personally, but keeping him assigned to the SCP. That's pushing against the line pretty hard.
3) The agent snaps and attacks the SCP with a shotgun, and the foundation goes "okay let's leave him here, but reprimand him a bit". This is the point where it's clearly past the canon line in my head.
4) The agent yet again snaps and enters the SCP in an unauthorised way, and what he gets is a reassignment and mandatory therapy. As if he'd smacked the ass off a secretary one too many times in the 80s, rather than acting in a flagrantly unprofessional and dangerous manner with regards to what is now known to be a dangerous SCP.

The entire thing is out of character for the foundation, and it just plain doesn't make sense given what little firmly established fiction there is. It reads like the story of a particularly dedicated parent in an unaffiliated horror story, or at best the story of a UIU agent with minimal supervision from his superiors. It does not, in any way shape or form, read like the actions of an SCP agent working on a supervised assignment with the foundation watching his every move.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sadly, drafted and coerced organizations are all too common, historically and even today. Look at recent events in the Congo for a prime, and tragic, example.

Yes, a volunteer force is better, more efficient, more loyal, less prone to disertion and betrayal, etc. But the Foundations administration is not going to pussy-foot around with a reluctant subject, not if they think he's needed for *dramatic sting music* The Most Important Mission In The World. They are protecting all life on Earth, and occasionally the stakes are even higher. Blackmail is the least of what they will stoop to.

Edit, Afterthoughts: There are also pros to conscription. For one, morale isn't a major concern. You know you will have zero, but it doesn't matter because the rank and file has to follow orders anyway. Under pain of whatever you used to coerce them in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How on earth is an organization made entirely up of people who have been blackmailed and possibly drugged into joining supposed to be effective? If you hate your employer, you're going to be much less effective. Plus, people would figure that out pretty quickly — "Wow, you were falsely accused of child porn too? Man, what a coincidence."

Seriously, if the Foundation isn't at least kinda nice to people who work for it, it won't exist.

item.title: Re: hey guys
item.content:encoded:
Are you sure you mean "canon"?
Because I'm getting that this violates your suspension of disbelief, a judgement call based on personal tastes and preferences. Which is a perfectly valid reason to dislike a piece, by the by. But to say something violates the canon is a more severe, and closer to objective, statement.

To violate what little canon we have around here would mean it changed the format, or claimed the Foundation was an official branch of some government, or that the Foundation has no military force, or some such contradiction of "fact".

item.title: Re: hey guys
item.content:encoded:
It's not a small detail, though, it's arguably the most important part of the entire story/article.
SCP canon is pretty loose and open to interpretation, but there is some level of detail that has to be present in each SCP article, otherwise you'd be able to write a generic horror story and then throw "…and then the foundation put it in a box." at the end and upload it as SCP-xxxx. No one on the site would accept that, and it's obvious why; this isn't a general creepypasta site, it's the SCP Foundation site.

This SCP is nowhere near as bad as the stupid example I just made up but it is, in my mind and apparently others, on the wrong side of the line. It's a good and entertaining story, but as it stands it is not a good and entertaining scp due to the various out-of-character and "canon" breaking reasons mentioned above and elsewhere in the discussion page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My point was that neither I or the family, if needed, really have a choice. I can see that the family had acted relatively peacefully (or did it?), not showing any affiliation with the foundation, but then again, as I've mentioned elsewhere, you have the conviction of the agent himself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Levels of Holy-Shitness were increasing sharply as the log went going and going… and it didn't stop. God, why didn't it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In reply to Snowshoe,
What makes you think that the odds aren't 50 to 1? Or 500 to 1, come to that. We never get a headcount on either the MTF or the mascots.

Let's assume that there are 20 soldiers, that's on the high end for a single squad but not unheard of IRL. Disney World has about 50,000 employees. It's also about ten times the acreage of 1357. The mascots are also filling the roles of other park-goers. So ballpark at somewhere north of 5,000 "Joe"s. That's a lot of assumptions, but even the most conservative estimates are going to have the Rangers outnumbered more than 100 to 1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does the Foundation give you a choice?

We are talking about an organization with access to unprecedented levels of influence. Some scientist whose skill-set they want doesn't want to leave his cushy tenure at Harvard? Oh, look, now he's accused of falsifying data and plagararizing papers, no one in academia is returning his calls, the bank decided to foreclose on his house, and the FBI is investigating reports of child pornography, etc. etc. Suddenly that job offer is looking pretty good, never mind that the contract is a bit strict and they are playing all cloak and dagger with the details. Probably just a defense contractor taking themselves too seriously, where do I sign?

That's not even considering that the common use of amnestics implies a mastery of pharma-physcology that makes MK-Ultra look like Flintstones Chewables.

Edit: Oops, meant to reply to Anax. Sorry for any confusion.

item.title: Re: hey guys
item.content:encoded:
I see the logic. Meaning, 682 is never meant to be killed, 106 never meant to be completely contained, 110 Montauk never really guessed because it's strength is in it's vagueness.

But I'm talking about the in-depth discussion of a, I hope this won't downgrade the SCP in any way, a small detail in a narrative story, while I've seen hate on people figuring out a big plot device as a whole. Everybody knows they can not guess it, so just let them as long they are not hurting anybody.

/rant :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I wouldn't work for an organisation that would kill me because of a clerical error to begin with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation almost assuredly has crippling morale problems to begin with. If word of that gets around, things get even worse. The Foundation's made up of people, people like us. Would you work for an organization that reserves the right to kill your wife and kids in return for your loyal service?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All it would take is a paragraph or two in your Foundation contract regarding the fact you have power of attorney for spouse and dependents, and are willing to surrender this if called for. Could explain why daddy was so focused on getting the job done. Never clarified how willing he was, nor his actual mood on that day. I mean, how many people actually read the entire contract? Benefits, Holidays, Bonus, blahblahblah…Scribble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The question here is: are they human?

If so, then standard riot tactics apply. Not counting possibility of ablative and/or kevlar armor in the costumes.

If not, then are they actually costumes, or the form they take. The ones in the logs all have somewhat mechanical personalities. More research/information is needed to define what they are.

Now for my questions. What kind of height/weight variations are there? We've seen male and female employees. Is each an individual, or are there 'templates' which repeat? What about the other research mission(s)? It was inferred that Agent Fredricks was able to piggyback on a mission he arranged. It also follows that we have observed at least five activations from the confirmed forms.

Since it takes one 'family', it would not be impossible to insert a large team in alongside them. From what was learned from this failure, they would have rules of engagement. "Have FUN. Don't push too hard. See if asking multiple employees…etc. As long as the parents don't get thrown out, we can use any number of tickets per month. Possibly with the same family, if they were not cycled D-class.

And we'll never know exactly what happened to Hailey. That was the kicker for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, we've all been assuming something that isn't neccessarily the case. Maybe the wife is Foundation also, just not an agent. Staff romances are reasonably common in the expanded non-canon, so it is a possibility. This doesn't change the fundamental question of if it was nuts or unreasonable to take their kid in, but it would explain some of your objections.

But anyway, rather than being a disqualifier I think that sort of sociopath is exactly the sort of agent the Foundation is looking for. A sane man can not cope with the insanity inherent in the job. Even if he started sane, the stress and incomprehensible horrors would get to him eventually.

Does he suck at interogation? Absolutely, probably the worst information gathering attempt on record. But that is utterly realistic. People screw-up. Particularly in high-stress situations and when they are doing something they aren't used to. Like, say, a combat operative being asked to gather information from a cartoon character while distracted worrying about his daughter and conflicted about his decision to put his loyalty to the cause over his duty to his family.

I'm not saying any of this is what actually happened, it's just one of several possible scenarios that could have played out behind the scenes of the records. Even if you or I find it morally reprehensible and obviously stupid, there are reasonable scenarios where plausible rationales would cause people to take these actions. People do evil and/or retarded things every day, so why is it unrealistic for the Foundation staff to engage in this universal human failing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking more along the lines of "Hey, we're sending this kid and civilian into this anomaly with minimal protection. This is the first time we're going to be testing with this object, so we should be prepared for any event that could reasonably occur. We've seen several hundred anomalous beings manifest spontaneously inside this place, so being prepared for all of them to attack seems reasonable. We should be prepared for this to happen while our testers are inside, since they'll probably be the ones tiggering (this was a typo, but it's not anymore) the event in the first place. Several armed guards and a standard riot control package have proven very effective in the past, and so should work here. Outfitting the testers so that they can effectively avoid being harmed by riot control seems logical."

As opposed to "Good luck, kid. Hope our task force (which is here specifically for this test that is taking place right here at this time) stationed fifteen minutes away can respond in time if you get eaten."

It's pretty hard to overrun somebody when you're being coated in a debatably lethal blinding gas, especially if they have guns. Unless the odds are fifty to one, a half blind man suffering from both a severe asthma attack and burning pain isn't going to take down a fit, trained, unhindered man with a gun. They're not trying to kill the people, they're just trying to get the kid, who could probably get away from Joe on her own. Getting somewhere she couldn't be found or getting to the exit wouldn't be hard after that.
